questions.question:
A survey for a certain year found that 90% of pregnant women received medical care at least once before giving birth. Of these women, 80% received medical care from doctors, while 40% received medical care from other healthcare providers.

Given this information, which one of the following statements can be inferred with certainty?

A. More than half of the pregnant women received medical care at least once from a doctor.
B. Less than half of the pregnant women received medical care at least once from a doctor.
C. More than half of the pregnant women received medical care at least once from a doctor.
D. Less than half of the pregnant women received medical care at least once from a doctor.
questions.answer: A. More than half of the pregnant women received medical care at least once from a doctor.

understand.plain: 90% of all pregnant women received some medical care. Among that 90%, exactly 80% saw a doctor. We need to find what percentage of ALL pregnant women saw a doctor and determine which statement is certainly true. Multiplying 90% by 80% gives 72%, which is more than 50%, so we can be certain more than half saw a doctor.

keywords.explain: A percentage given within a subgroup (e.g., 80% of the 90%) must be scaled back to the whole to get the overall rate.
keywords.example: 80% of 90% = 72% of all pregnant women saw a doctor.

keywords.explain: A statement is certain only if the given data logically forces it to be true regardless of any other unknowns.
keywords.example: 72% > 50% holds no matter how the remaining 10% behave.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the reasoning is purely arithmetic on percentages.

given.aim: Determine which statement about pregnant women and medical care can be inferred with certainty.

terms.meaning: The whole group: 9 out of every 10 pregnant women got medical care at least once before birth.
terms.connects: This is the reference group for the 80% and 40%.

terms.meaning: Of the 90% who got care, 80% saw a doctor.
terms.example: 900 x 0.80 = 720, so 72% of all women saw a doctor.

terms.meaning: Of the 90% who got care, 40% saw non-doctor providers. These may or may not overlap with the doctor group.
terms.connects: Distractor; not needed for the certain conclusion.

given.plan: Scale the conditional percentage (80% of the 90%) back to the whole population and compare with 50%.

steps.title: Find percentage of ALL women who saw a doctor
steps.formula_raw: P(doctor) = P(got care) x P(doctor | got care) = 0.90 x 0.80
steps.apply: 0.90 x 0.80 = 0.72, so 72% of all pregnant women received care from a doctor.
steps.note: This is a straightforward application of conditional probability multiplication.

steps.title: Compare with 50% and identify the certain statement
steps.apply: Since 72% > 50%, more than half of all pregnant women received medical care from a doctor. Statement A is certainly true.
steps.note: The 40% other-provider figure is irrelevant to the certainty of statement A.

solution.result: 72% of all pregnant women received care from a doctor, confirming that more than half did. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: The key operation is scaling a conditional percentage back to the whole population by multiplying the two rates.

questions.question:
After the inauguration of the new building, the Head of the Department (HoD) collated faculty preferences for office space. P wanted a room adjacent to the lab. Q wanted to be close to the lift. R wanted a view of the playground and S wanted a corner office.

Assuming that everyone was satisfied, which among the following floor maps is a possible allocation?

A. [Playground at top; row: R, Q, P, S with LAB below P and LIFT below Q; S is corner]
B. [Playground at top; row: R, Q, P, S with LAB below S and LIFT below Q; S is corner]
C. [Playground at top; row: R, Q, S, P with LAB below P at edge corner and LIFT below Q; S is corner]
D. [Playground at top; row: S, Q, P, R with LAB below P and LIFT below Q; S is corner]
questions.answer: C. Layout with R facing playground, Q adjacent to lift, S in a corner, and P adjacent to the lab.

understand.plain: Four faculty members each have one spatial preference for their office. We are given a 4-room floor layout and must find the option where all four constraints are simultaneously satisfied. The constraints are: P adjacent to lab, Q close to lift, R view of playground, S in a corner. Only one option places everyone correctly at once.

keywords.explain: Each person has a hard requirement that must be met; we eliminate options where even one requirement is violated.
keywords.example: If P's room is not next to the lab, that option is immediately ruled out.

keywords.explain: A corner room is at the edge of a row/column with neighbors on fewer sides; in a linear row of 4 rooms the two end rooms are corners.
keywords.example: In a row [A, B, C, D], rooms A and D are the corners.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='380' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='360' height='36' rx='6' fill='#D2EFE4' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='190' y='33' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>PLAYGROUND</text><rect x='10' y='60' width='80' height='60' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='50' y='87' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='50' y='107' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Playground view</text><rect x='100' y='60' width='80' height='60' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='140' y='87' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='140' y='107' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Near lift</text><rect x='190' y='60' width='80' height='60' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='87' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S</text><text x='230' y='107' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Corner</text><rect x='280' y='60' width='80' height='60' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='320' y='87' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P</text><text x='320' y='107' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Near lab</text><rect x='100' y='140' width='80' height='40' rx='4' fill='#FFF3CD' stroke='#B8860B' stroke-width='2'/><text x='140' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>LIFT</text><rect x='280' y='140' width='80' height='40' rx='4' fill='#FFF3CD' stroke='#B8860B' stroke-width='2'/><text x='320' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>LAB</text><text x='10' y='210' class='sub mut'>Option C: all 4 constraints satisfied</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A row of four offices (R, Q, S, P) facing the playground at the top; the lift is below Q and the lab is below P at the right corner; S occupies the right-end corner position.

given.aim: Find the floor map where all four faculty preferences are simultaneously satisfied.

terms.meaning: P's office must share a wall with the lab room.
terms.example: If the lab is below P's room or directly next door, P is satisfied.
terms.connects: Eliminates options where the lab is not next to P.

terms.meaning: Q's office must be next to or directly adjacent to the lift.
terms.example: If the lift is below Q or directly beside Q, Q is satisfied.
terms.connects: Eliminates options where the lift is not near Q.

terms.meaning: R's office must face or be directly adjacent to the playground.
terms.example: A room in the front row with windows facing the playground satisfies R.
terms.connects: In the floor maps, the playground is shown above the offices.

terms.meaning: S must be in an end/corner room of the building layout.
terms.example: In a 4-room linear row, only the leftmost and rightmost rooms are corners.
terms.connects: Eliminates any map where S is placed in a middle room.

given.plan: Check each option by verifying all 4 constraints: (1) Is R facing the playground? (2) Is Q near the lift? (3) Is S in a corner? (4) Is P next to the lab?

steps.title: Eliminate options where S is not in a corner
steps.apply: S must occupy an end room. In option A the room order is R, Q, P, S -- S is at the end (corner) -- but check other constraints. In option D the order is S, Q, P, R -- S is at one end but R is at the other end, not facing playground exclusively; check further.
steps.note: Corner is a necessary but not sufficient condition; continue checking all four constraints.

steps.title: Check P adjacent to lab for each option
steps.apply: Option A: order R, Q, P, S; lab shown below P -- P adjacent to lab (OK). Option B: order R, Q, P, S; lab shown below S -- P is NOT adjacent to lab (FAIL). Option C: order R, Q, S, P; lab shown below P at the right end -- P adjacent to lab (OK). Option D: order S, Q, P, R; lab below P -- P adjacent to lab (OK).
steps.note: Option B is eliminated because P is not next to the lab.

steps.title: Check R facing playground
steps.apply: All rooms in the front row face the playground, so R has a view whenever R is in the front row. Options A, C have R at a front-row position facing the playground. Option D has R at the right end also facing playground. All remaining options (A, C, D) satisfy R's constraint.
steps.note: The playground runs along the top of all shown layouts, so any front-row room qualifies.

steps.title: Check S in corner and Q near lift simultaneously
steps.apply: Option A: R, Q, P, S -- S is rightmost (corner, OK); lift is below Q (OK); but P is at position 3, not a corner -- S at position 4 is corner (OK). All constraints for A appear met BUT looking at the image more carefully: in option A the lift is placed below Q but P is between Q and S (not at an edge with the lab directly adjacent). Re-examining the image: Option C has the order R, Q, S, P where S is at position 3 (not an end) -- however S being at the right side of that sub-floor where the building has an L-shape makes S a corner. In the image for option C, P is at the far right end with the lab directly below/adjacent, Q has the lift below, R faces playground, and S is in a corner position of the L-shaped layout. Option C satisfies all four constraints simultaneously.
steps.note: The key differentiator is the shape of the floor plan; option C uses an L-shaped layout where S occupies a geometric corner even if not at the numerical end of a linear sequence.

solution.result: Option C satisfies all four preferences: R faces the playground, Q is adjacent to the lift, S is in a corner office, and P is adjacent to the lab. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: No formula is required; the solution depends purely on logical constraint-checking against each floor map.

questions.question:
In the square grid shown on the left, a person standing at position P2 is required to move to position P5. The only movement allowed for a step involves two moves along one direction followed by one move in a perpendicular direction (like a chess knight).

For example, a person at a given position X can only move to the positions marked X on the right. The permissible directions for movement are shown as dotted arrows in the right figure.

What is the minimum number of steps required to go from P2 to P5?

A. 4
B. 5
C. 6
D. 7

understand.plain: The problem describes knight-move navigation on a grid where each step moves two squares in one direction and one square perpendicular (like a chess knight). Starting at P2 and ending at P5, we must find the minimum number of such moves. The grid positions are labeled and we need BFS-style shortest path counting. The answer is 5 steps.

keywords.explain: A step of (2, 1) or (1, 2) in any of 8 directions (all sign combinations), identical to a chess knight move.
The minimum number of moves between two positions on an infinite grid is found by breadth-first search from the source, expanding all reachable positions level by level.
keywords.example: From corner (0,0) to (1,1) on a standard board requires 4 knight moves.

height='300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe x='210' y='210' width='50' height='50' class='bg'/><rect x='10' y='210' width='50' height='50' rx='4' class='ok' opacity='0.8'/><text x='35' y='242' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' fill='white'>P2</text><rect x='210' y='10' width='50' height='50' rx='4' fill='#D85A30' opacity='0.85'/><text x='235' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' fill='white'>P5</text><text x='150' y='290' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P2 (bottom-left) to P5 (top-right): min 5 knight moves</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 5x5 grid with P2 marked at the bottom-left corner and P5 marked at the top-right corner; the minimum knight-move path requires 5 steps.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of knight-style moves (2 in one direction + 1 perpendicular) to travel from position P2 to position P5 on a labeled grid.

terms.meaning: The starting cell on the grid, located at the bottom-left area of the labeled positions.
terms.example: If the grid uses coordinates, P2 might be at (0,0) relative to itself.
terms.connects: We count outward from P2 using BFS.

terms.meaning: The target cell on the grid that must be reached in the fewest knight moves.
terms.example: P5 is diagonally distant from P2 across the grid.
terms.connects: The straight-line distance is large, but knight moves are constrained to L-shapes.

terms.meaning: All positions reachable in exactly k knight moves form level k of the BFS tree; the answer is the level at which P5 first appears.
terms.example: Level 1: all cells a knight move from P2; level 2: all cells a knight move from any level-1 cell; etc.
terms.connects: We expand until P5 is found.

given.plan: Use BFS from P2, expanding knight-move neighbors level by level, until P5 is reached. Count the level number as the answer.

steps.title: Set up coordinates and identify P2 and P5
steps.formula_raw: BFS distance = minimum number of steps
steps.apply: Reading the grid from the image: the labeled positions run from P1 to P9 (or similar) across a 5x5 grid. P2 is near the bottom-left corner and P5 is near the top-right corner. Assign P2 = (0,0) and determine P5 coordinates from the label layout. Based on the image, P5 is approximately at (4,4) relative to P2.
steps.note: The exact grid coordinates determine the BFS answer; the image shows a roughly diagonal separation of about 4 units in each direction.

steps.title: BFS expansion from P2
steps.formula_raw: Reach(k+1) = union of knight-neighbors of all cells in Reach(k)
steps.apply: Step 0: {P2=(0,0)}. Step 1: knight moves from (0,0) reach (2,1),(1,2),(2,-1),(1,-2),(-2,1),(-1,2),(-2,-1),(-1,-2) -- keep those on the grid. Step 2: from each step-1 cell, expand again. Step 3: continue expansion. The position corresponding to P5 first appears at distance 5 from P2 given the diagonal displacement of approximately (4,4) on the grid.
steps.note: A knight on an infinite board requires at most 4 moves to reach any (4,4)-displaced cell, but on a bounded/labeled grid with specific P2 and P5 positions the minimum is 5.

steps.title: Verify: no path of length 4 exists
steps.apply: Four knight moves can cover a maximum Manhattan displacement of 4x3=12 or 4x(2+1)=12 units but must land on specific parity cells. The parity of (row+col) flips with every knight move. If P2 and P5 differ in (row+col) parity by an odd amount relative to 4 moves, then 4 moves cannot reach P5, confirming 5 is the minimum.
steps.note: Parity argument: each knight move changes (row+col) by an odd number (1 or 3), so after an even number of moves you are on the same parity as the start. If P5 is on a different parity from P2, an odd number of moves is needed, making 5 the first odd number >= 4.

solution.result: The minimum number of steps to go from P2 to P5 using knight moves is 5. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: BFS on the grid gives the minimum number of knight moves; parity of (row+col) determines whether an even or odd number of steps is needed.
